title: US Sea Drones Make Combat Debut Against Iran, Germany Buys 50,000 FPV Drones for Ukraine, UK Pours £5.4B Into Typhoon Fleet
subhead: The US military used Saronic Corsair unmanned surface vessels to strike Iran's Bandar Abbas naval base — the first combat deployment of American sea drones. Germany funded a €90M order for 50,000 AI-guided FPV drones for Ukraine, while the UK committed £5.4B to upgrade its entire Typhoon fleet.

sections.heading: US Deploys Sea Drones in Combat for the First Time, Striking Iran's Bandar Abbas
sections.body:
CENTCOM confirmed that three Saronic Corsair unmanned surface vessels were used in a strike on a submarine and ship maintenance facility at Iran's Bandar Abbas Naval Base on July 13, marking what the Pentagon called the first time American forces have employed sea drones in combat operations. Video released by CENTCOM shows the vessels racing toward the port before striking naval infrastructure.

The Corsair, built by US firm Saronic Technologies, is a 24-foot USV with a range exceeding 1,000 nautical miles, a payload capacity of 1,000 pounds, and a top speed above 35 knots, according to the manufacturer. It carries an embedded AI stack and open architecture for rapid sensor and autonomy integration.

The strike came during the third consecutive night of US operations against Iran, after President Trump declared the ceasefire over and reinstated a blockade on Iranian shipping through the Strait of Hormuz. CENTCOM said the attack degraded Iran's ability to continue attacking commercial shipping through the strait.

The Corsair had previously been used operationally — but not in a strike role — when one rescued two Army Apache helicopter crew members near the Strait of Hormuz in June. The vessels are operated by Unmanned Task Group 59.1 under Task Force 59, which the Navy stood up in January 2024 specifically to stress-test unmanned surface vessels in the Middle East. Their mission has now shifted decisively from surveillance to strike.
sections.whyItMatters: The first US combat use of sea drones marks a turning point for unmanned naval warfare. Ukraine's sea drones reshaped the Black Sea; the US is now replicating that playbook in the Persian Gulf. Saronic's Corsair went from surveillance to one-way attack missions in under six months, demonstrating how fast unmanned platforms can transition from testing to kinetic operations.

sections.heading: Germany Funds 50,000 AI-Guided FPV Drones for Ukraine in €90M Deal
sections.body:
Germany is financing the purchase of 50,000 Shrike first-person-view (FPV) drones for Ukraine under a contract worth approximately €90 million ($103 million), according to Reuters — one of the largest known single drone orders by a Western government for Kyiv.

The drones are manufactured by Ukrainian firm SkyFall and equipped with autonomous targeting software from US defense technology company Auterion. Auterion CEO Lorenz Meier confirmed the contract's value and said some drones have already been delivered, with the remainder expected this year.

The Shrike platform has combat-proven capability: a variant known as the Shrike 10-F, developed jointly by SkyFall and British company Skycutter, recently topped the first round of a Pentagon competition under a $1.1 billion program to procure hundreds of thousands of one-way attack drones. Auterion also delivered 33,000 AI drone strike kits under a separate $50 million Pentagon contract in 2025.

This order is part of a broader Western drone pipeline. Meier said Auterion is helping deliver a total of 100,000 drones to Ukraine this year across multiple manufacturers and government-funded contracts. The UK separately pledged 150,000 drones this year as part of a £752 million military assistance package.
sections.whyItMatters: The scale and speed of this deal shows how drone procurement has shifted from boutique programs to mass production. Germany's €90M for a single order of AI-guided FPVs — and Auterion's 100,000-drone annual delivery target — signal that autonomous loitering munitions are now treated as munitions, not platforms, with supply chains to match.

sections.heading: Ukraine's Sea Drone Campaign Halts All Shipping in the Sea of Azov
sections.body:
Ukraine's Unmanned Systems Forces struck more than 105 Russian vessels between July 6 and July 13, according to brigade commander Robert Brovdi, forcing Russia to completely halt shipping through the Sea of Azov and the Don River channel. Reuters confirmed the shipping shutdown, and satellite imagery from Copernicus Sentinel showed burning vessels in the Kerch Strait area.

The campaign targets ship bridges — the command-and-control centers — to achieve what Campbell University professor and merchant mariner Salvatore Mercogliano called 'mission kills': forcing crew abandonment without necessarily sinking the vessel. According to the Institute for the Study of War, most large Russian ships have cleared out of the Sea of Azov entirely, with a cluster of roughly 25 remaining vessels sheltering in the northeast.

The shutdown has immediate economic consequences: Reuters reports the Sea of Azov route carries approximately one-quarter of Russian grain exports, and wheat prices have already started rising. The campaign has also deepened the isolation of occupied Crimea by cutting off seaborne fuel deliveries, which is already under severe rationing due to Ukraine's long-range drone strikes on Russian refineries.
sections.whyItMatters: A country without a conventional navy has effectively closed a major commercial sea lane using unmanned surface vessels. The Sea of Azov shutdown demonstrates that sea drones can achieve strategic blockade effects at a fraction of the cost of traditional naval power — and that the model the US just emulated against Iran was first proven by Ukraine against Russia.

sections.heading: France Licenses Ukraine to Produce SCALP, Aster, and AASM Missiles
sections.body:
French President Emmanuel Macron announced at the Coalition of the Willing summit in Paris on July 13 that France will grant Ukraine licenses to produce SCALP cruise missiles, Aster 30 interceptors for SAMP/T air defense systems, and AASM guided bombs. Ukraine also plans to purchase 16 Rafale fighter jets, with Macron saying they would begin flying missions in Ukrainian skies in 2028–2029.

The license announcement follows President Trump's pledge at the NATO Ankara summit last week to allow Ukraine to produce Patriot interceptors — meaning Ukraine now has production licensing commitments for both European and American air defense missile systems. However, experts caution that serial production cannot be launched quickly; the NPR notes that even with a license, establishing domestic manufacturing lines for complex missiles takes years.

The same Paris summit also produced a new Integrated Anti-Ballistic Missile Coalition among Ukraine and nine European countries — Germany, France, Italy, Spain, the Netherlands, Sweden, Norway, Denmark, and the UK. Zelensky said the coalition aims to develop a mass-produced, low-cost anti-ballistic system within 12 months, leveraging Ukraine's combat experience against Russian missile attacks.
sections.whyItMatters: The French license decision, combined with Trump's Patriot pledge, represents a fundamental shift in Western arms policy: from donating finished weapons to transferring production know-how. If implemented, Ukraine would become the only country outside the manufacturer's home nations licensed to build Aster, SCALP, and Patriot missiles — a move designed to build sustainable air defense capacity but one that raises serious questions about technology security and production timelines.

sections.heading: UK Commits £5.4B to Upgrade Entire Typhoon Fleet as Macron Warns Against European Fragmentation
sections.body:
The Royal Air Force will upgrade all 107 Eurofighter Typhoon Tranche 2 and 3 aircraft at a cost of £5.4 billion, up significantly from the 40 aircraft previously planned for upgrade in January 2026. Minister for Defense Readiness Luke Pollard confirmed the investment includes radar, communications, software upgrades, new defensive aids systems, and improvements to weapons systems including an upgraded helmet-mounted sight.

The UK has not placed new Typhoon orders, unlike Germany, Spain, and Italy. Instead, it is extending the fleet's service life into the 2040s through the Long Term Evolution program, supplemented by £8.6 billion for the GCAP/Tempest 6th-generation fighter, £2.2 billion for F-35s, and £300 million for Collaborative Combat Aircraft loyal wingman drones.

In Paris, outgoing French President Emmanuel Macron used his final address to the armed forces to warn against European defense fragmentation. 'Wherever nationalism is pandered to, whether in France or elsewhere, we are misunderstanding our own history,' Macron said, noting that France's military budget has doubled from €32 billion in 2017 to a projected €64 billion in 2027, reaching 2.2% of GDP this year — still well short of NATO's 2035 target of 3.5%.
sections.whyItMatters: The UK's decision to upgrade its entire Typhoon fleet rather than buy new ones reflects a broader European dilemma: sustaining current platforms while developing next-generation fighters. Macron's farewell speech underscored the political tension — Europe is rearming, but doing it through national programs rather than joint procurement, threatening to duplicate rather than multiply capability.

sections.heading: Lockheed Wins $850M for Trident II Life Extension as Nuclear Triad Modernization Presses On
sections.body:
Lockheed Martin received an $850 million contract modification for the Trident II D5 Life Extension 2 (D5LE2) program, continuing development of the submarine-launched ballistic missile that forms the sea-based leg of the US nuclear triad. Work will run through September 2030, primarily at Lockheed facilities in Colorado and Florida.

The D5LE2 program ensures the Trident system remains effective through 2084, and will be integrated with the forthcoming Columbia-class ballistic missile submarines entering service in the 2030s. Lockheed is the primary contractor for production, assembly, sustainment, and life extension; General Dynamics Mission Systems handles fire control; Northrop Grumman supplies propulsion.

The contract comes amid the broader nearly $1 trillion US nuclear modernization program. As an Atlantic analysis this week noted, the US is spending vast sums modernizing its arsenal without a clear updated nuclear posture — the Trump administration has defaulted to the 2018 Nuclear Posture Review rather than issuing an update, despite two ongoing wars involving nuclear-armed powers.
sections.whyItMatters: The Trident extension is a routine but significant piece of the slowest-moving modernization program in the Pentagon. What's notable is the context: the US is spending nearly a trillion dollars on nuclear modernization while simultaneously fighting a conventional war in Iran, with no updated doctrine to explain what the arsenal is for beyond strategic deterrence — the same gap the Cold War revealed.

sections.heading: Korea-US-Japan Sign SMR Pact to Counter Chinese and Russian Nuclear Export Dominance
sections.body:
South Korea, the United States, and Japan signed a memorandum of cooperation on small modular reactor (SMR) deployment on the sidelines of the NATO summit in Ankara on July 7. The agreement aims to jointly support SMR introduction in the Indo-Pacific and pursue global market opportunities, with Seoul viewing it as both an industrial alliance and a strategic counterweight to Chinese and Russian nuclear supply chains.

A South Korean Foreign Ministry official said the three countries offer 'complementary strengths': the US leads in SMR technology but hasn't built a commercial nuclear plant in decades; Japan's supply chain shrank after Fukushima; and South Korea brings construction and manufacturing capability with a track record of on-schedule, on-budget delivery. The partnership targets the Indo-Pacific, expected to see the largest increase in new nuclear plant demand, before lower-cost Chinese and Russian projects become entrenched.

Beyond market competition, Seoul hopes the agreement will strengthen its case for expanding civilian nuclear cooperation with Washington, including greater autonomy over uranium enrichment — a sensitive issue given that commercial high-assay low-enriched uranium production remains largely limited to Russia.
sections.whyItMatters: SMRs are the next frontier of nuclear competition, and the US-Japan-Korea pact is an attempt to offer an alternative to Rosatom's dominance before China's SMR program scales. But the agreement also touches the proliferation seam: Seoul wants enrichment capabilities that Washington has historically restricted, and the deal's strategic value depends on whether the three partners can actually align industrial policy rather than just sign memoranda.

bottomLine: Three developments stood out on July 14: the US military's first-ever combat use of sea drones against Iran signals that unmanned naval warfare has moved from experimentation to operational strikes; Germany's €90M order for 50,000 AI-guided FPV drones for Ukraine shows mass autonomous munitions procurement becoming routine; and France's decision to license Ukraine to produce SCALP, Aster, and AASM missiles represents a shift from donating finished weapons to transferring production technology. Meanwhile, the UK chose fleet longevity over new Typhoon orders, Lockheed pressed ahead with an $850M nuclear missile extension, and a Korea-US-Japan SMR pact opened a new front in the competition for nuclear export markets against China and Russia.
sources: Military Times, CENTCOM, News18, Stars and Stripes, Saronic Technologies, Reuters, Kyiv Post, Firstpost, Auterion, SkyFall, Ars Technica, Institute for the Study of War, Meduza, Los Angeles Times, Politico EU, NPR, MercoPress, American Machinist, The Atlantic, Korea Herald, US Navy, Lockheed Martin
